content.full_text:
May 9, 1990

John Wallace, Attorney at Law  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
P.O. Box 807  
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Wallace:

At your request, this is to memorialize our conversation on the afternoon of May 7, 1990, concerning a conflict of interest question that you and I discussed.

As I related to you, a member of my City Council is an electrical contractor who owns an interest in a contracting business in the same city. The City council is considering how to treat certain illegal dwelling units, including whether to adopt some new regulations that would permit such units to remain if they are brought up to building code and habitability standards. If such a proposal were adopted, some property owners would probably upgrade those illegal dwelling units, and this would generate business in the electrical contracting field.

You and I discussed In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198 and agreed that the case was relevant but not dispositive. We tentatively agreed that it would be too speculative to conclude at this point that the Councilmember/electrical contractor would have a conflict of interest that would disqualify him from participating in the Council's policy decision. This is because we could not yet identify any specific units or properties that would generate such electrical contracting business, as well as because there are a great many electrical contractors in the Culver City/Los Angeles area who would be potential competitors for whatever business was generated.

We further agreed that at some point events might occur that would change the situation, making a material financial

John Wallace, Attorney at Law  
May 9, 1990  
Page 2

effect reasonably foreseeable, but that for now such an effect was more speculative than reasonably foreseeable.

Thank you very much for your time and your help.

Very truly yours,

ELEANOR M. EGAN  
City Attorney
